data.title: Dutch government in talks with Nvidia for AI facility in the Netherlands - report
data.description:
The government of the Netherlands is in discussions with Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) to support the setting up of an AI facility in the country, Bloomberg News reported.

The facility will be aimed at the development and application of AI with the help of a supercomputer, the report added, citing a statement. "Discussions were held with Nvidia about the delivery of hardware and support with knowledge in creating the required infrastructure."

The Dutch government has reached a deal with Nvidia for the supply of hardware and technological knowledge for the potential AI facility, according to a report from Reuters.

Nvidia and AMD have not yet responded to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Dutch Economic Affairs Minister Dirk Beljaarts is on a visit to the U.S. to boost cooperation between technology companies of the two countries.

Beljaarts said the chances of realizing the project grew significantly through the agreement with Nvidia, without providing details, the report added.

"Competition is fierce, the whole world is after this technology," said Beljaarts following a meeting with Nvidia, the report noted.

data.title: Euro Area retail sales tick 1.2% higher in November
data.description:
Retail sales in the Euro Area increased 0.10% in November over the previous month, missing consensus estimates of 0.4%. 

Y/Y retail sales increased 1.20% in November over the same month in the previous year. 

Trade volume for food, drinks, tobacco increased by 0.1%, non-food products (except automotive fuel) was 0.6% higher, automotive fuel in specialized stores was up by 0.8%.

data.title: Adentra strengthens leadership with board additions, COO appointment
data.description:
• Adentra (TSX:ADEN:CA) Wednesday reported the retirement of founding director Graham Wilson, new board appointments, and the promotion of Drew Dickinson to Chief Operating Officer.
  • Rob Taylor, who has been a director of Adentra since 2019, will become Chair of the Board effective immediately.
  • Drew Dickinson, with over 30 years of industry and leadership experience, has been promoted to Chief Operating Officer of Adentra.
  • Dickinson served as COO of Rugby Architectural Building Products prior to its acquisition by Adentra, President of Rugby, and most recently as President of Adentra's U.S. Industrial business.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Zoomcar reports 17% Y/Y growth in December 2024 bookings
data.description:
• India-based car sharing marketplace Zoomcar Holdings (NASDAQ:ZCAR) announced on Thursday a 17% year-over-year growth in bookings for December 2024.
  • The company said it recorded its highest-ever unaudited and unreviewed contribution profit for December 2024, amounting to $494.51K based on provisional financials for the month.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: JCPenney, Aéropostale owner merge to form Catalyst Brands
data.description:
JCPenney, a department store chain owned by Simon Property Group (NYSE:SPG) and Brookfield Asset Management (NYSE:BAM), has merged with SPARC Group to form a new company called Catalyst Brands.

The joint venture will house SPARC's brands Aéropostale, Brooks Brothers, Eddie Bauer, Lucky Brand and Nautica along with JCPenney and its brands.

Catalyst Brands was formed in an all-equity deal between JCPenney and SPARC, with shareholders Simon Property (NYSE:SPG), Brookfield (NYSE:BAM), Authentic Brands and Shein. Financial terms were not disclosed.

"Catalyst Brands launches with more than $9B of revenue, 1,800 store locations, 60,000 employees and $1B of liquidity," JCPenney said in a statement. The firm will integrate complementary strengths, including strong product design and growing use of data-driven and AI technology to enhance its supply chain, inventory management and customer relationships.

SPARC is a partnership that includes Authentic Brands Group (which owns brands like Reebok and Forever 21), Simon Property (NYSE:SPG) and fast-fashion shopping app Shein.

Notably, Catalyst Brands has sold Reebok's U.S. operations and is exploring strategic options for Forever 21. The Catalyst Brands deal does not affect the intellectual property owned by Authentic Brands that SPARC Group licenses.

JCPenney CEO Marc Rosen will lead Catalyst Brands, with three brand CEOs overseeing its portfolio. Former Walmart executive Kevin Harper will join Catalyst Brands as COO.

data.title: Arm Holdings eyes Oracle-backed Ampere Computing acquisition - report
data.description:
SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY) and its majority-owned subsidiary, Arm Holdings (NASDAQ:ARM), are exploring a potential acquisition of Oracle (ORCL)-backed semiconductor company Ampere Computing, Bloomberg reported on Thursday, citing people with knowledge of the matter.

Ampere Computing is a Santa Clara, California-based semiconductor company that makes processors for data center machinery using Arm’s (ARM) technology. Amepre was valued at ~$8B in a proposed minority investment by SoftBank Group (OTCPK:SFTBY) in 2021.

The California-based company, backed by Oracle (ORCL) and Larry Ellison, had previously considered going public and had also collaborated with a financial adviser to field takeover interest from larger industry players in September.

Ampere seems to be focused on finding a strategic buyer to enhance its growth prospects in a competitive landscape dominated by major players like Intel (INTC) and AMD (AMD). This may likely indicate that Ampere does not foresee an IPO as an easy path.

While discussions are ongoing, sources indicate that negotiations could still fall through, and Ampere may attract interest from other potential buyers as well, the report highlighted.

Arm Holdings (ARM), Ampere Computing, SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY) and Oracle (ORCL) did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

Arm (ARM) stock traded 0.48% lower on Wednesday during after-market hours of trading, while Oracle (ORCL) was down 0.33%.

data.title: JPMorgan files two lawsuits related to Greek fintech Viva Wallet investment - report
data.description:
• JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM) has filed two lawsuits in connection with the financial services company's investment in the Greek fintech Viva Wallet, Reuters reported, citing a source with knowledge of the matter and an emailed statement.
  • The bank had bought a 48.5% stake in Viva for ~$800M in 2022. Holdings and investment company WeRealize owns 51.49%, the report noted.
  • A €916M lawsuit is said to have been filed in Greece on January 2 over the valuation of Viva, the latest step in the legal dispute between the two sides.
  • JPM also filed a case against Werealize in the U.K. this week, alleging the latter breached shareholder agreements, the source reportedly said.

data.title: European markets mixed amid inflation concerns, German trade surplus widens
data.description:
London (UKX) +0.4%

Germany (DAX:IND) -0.1%

Germany's trade surplus widened to EUR 19.7 billion in November, surpassing forecasts of EUR 14.8 billion.

Industrial production in Germany increased 1.5% month-over-month in November, following an upwardly revised 0.4% fall in October.

France (CAC:IND) flat

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) ticked -0.2% lower on Thursday, with retail stocks at the forefront of the selloff, as investor focused on the inflation outlook.

Other economically sensitive sectors such as banks, insurers and automakers also fell as German 10-year bond yields touched their highest since July 11.

Trading volumes are expected to be lighter than usual, with U.S. markets closed to mark a national day of mourning following the death of former President Jimmy Carter.

Coming up in the session: Euro Area retail sales awaited shortly.

In the bond market, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was down 2 basis points to 4.67%.

Germany's 10-year yield was down less than 1 basis point to 2.52%.

UK's 10-year yield was up 3 basis points t0 4.83%.

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (EWG), (GF), (EWI), (EWQ), (FGM), (DAX), (FLGR), (FXB), (EWU), (FKU), (EWUS), (FLGB), (GREK)

data.title: Jimmy Carter funeral: Here’s a rundown of the financial markets that are open and closed
data.description:
As the nation remembers Jimmy Carter, the New York Stock Exchange and Nasdaq are closed Thursday, with stock trading resuming Friday on a normal schedule. The U.S. bond market will close early at 2:00 p.m. Eastern Time.

The labor data, normally released on Thursdays, reported Wednesday with a 10K drop to 201K, while EIA Natural Gas reported a decline in inventory by 40 bcf.

Trading in energy and metals futures are open for their normal hours Thursday.

Thursday is a National Day of Mourning in honor of Carter. A state funeral for Carter, who died on Dec. 29 at age 100, will be held Thursday morning at Washington National Cathedral. The last presidential funeral was in 2018 for George Bush.

U.S. stock index futures are open until 9:30 a.m. ET, and reopening Thursday evening at 6 p.m. ET as normal. All the benchmark indices are trading lower, Nasdaq Futures (US100:IND) trading 0.47% lower, S&P 500 Futures (SPX) down 0.36%, and Dow Jones Futures (INDU) shedding 0.41%, at the time of writing.

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) is down 3% at 93,110. United States 2-Year Bond Yield (US2Y) shed 0.37%, while United States 10-Year Bond Yield (US10Y) is down 0.5%.

U.S. stocks whipsawed through a volatile trading session on Wednesday to finally end mixed, as investors refrained from big moves ahead of a stock market closure and the highly anticipated December nonfarm payrolls report on Friday. The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) added +0.16% to finish at 5,918.23 points, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) climbed +0.25% to settle at 42,635.20 points. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) slipped -0.06% to conclude at 19,478.88 points.

data.title: Yellen: COVID stimulus may have contributed a little bit to higher inflation
data.description:
The $1.9T stimulus package to support the U.S. recovery from the effects of the COVID-19 pandemic may have contributed "a little bit" to the elevated inflation that followed, Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen told CNBC in an interview.

But Yellen insisted the stimulus package, signed into law by President Joe Biden in 2021, was necessary. "We had a pandemic that was raging out of control, thousands of people were dying. And the unemployment rate remained extremely high."

"It may have contributed a little bit to the inflation. But by and large, the inflation was a supply-side phenomenon," she said, pointing to the pandemic causing shortages of critical goods. "I think an important factor in the inflation was that these shortages started pushing up prices a great deal."

Yellen noted that currently, the CPI rate is a little bit above 2%. "We haven't seen too much progress in the last couple of months. But I feel pretty convinced that we're on a downward path. The labor market has cooled and is in a very similar good state like it was prior to the pandemic."

As for the U.S. fiscal deficit, Yellen said higher interest rates have pushed up the cost of servicing outstanding debt. "That's one factor that's been involved, but discretionary spending is at historically low levels."

When asked about the Department of Government Efficiency's goal of cutting federal spending by $2T, Yellen said, "it's hard to see how the math on that works."

President-elect Donald Trump has nominated hedge fund manager Scott Bessent to replace Yellen. "I'm pleased to see somebody with experience who will be taking over presumably if confirmed by the Senate," she said.
